44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown

123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).

30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.

104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122

124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.

131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.

38	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
39	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
40	
41	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
42	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
43	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
44	\[
45	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
46	  \qquad
47	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
48	\]
49	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
50	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
51	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
52	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
55	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
56	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
57	% groups)
58	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
59	% SOURCE QUOTE:
60	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
61	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
62	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
63	% abelian groups defined by the formula
64	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
65	% \end{dfn}
66	%  [\dots]
67	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
68	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
69	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
70	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
71	% topologies  by
72	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
73	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
74	%  \end{dfn}"
75	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
76	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
77	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
78	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
79	homomorphisms.}
80	
81	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
82	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
83	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
84	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
85	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
86	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
87	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
88	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
89	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
90	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
91	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
92	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
93	structure. This is the content of
94	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
95	
96	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
97	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
98	\[
99	  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
100	  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
101	\]
102	on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
103	Lean carrier:
104	\begin{enumerate}
105	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
106	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
107	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
108	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
109	    for \(\otimes\);
110	  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
111	    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
112	    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
113	\end{enumerate}
114	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
115	available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
116	binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
117	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
118	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
119	(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
120	
121	\section{Mathlib API survey}
122	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
123	
124	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
125	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
128	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
129	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
130	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
131	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
132	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
133	sectionwise tensor product
134	\[
135	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
136	\]
137	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
138	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
139	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
140	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
141	
142	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
144	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
145	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
147	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
148	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
149	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
150	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
151	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
152	tensor-product operation
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
154	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
155	
156	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
157	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
158	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
159	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
160	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
162	unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
163	\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
164	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
165	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
166	natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
167	parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
168	on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
169	the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.
170	
171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
216	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
218	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
219	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
220	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
221	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
222	  \[
223	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
224	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
225	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
226	  \]
227	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
228	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
229	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
230	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
231	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
232	  isomorphisms
233	  \[
234	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	    \qquad
236	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	  \]
238	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
239	  \[
240	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
241	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
242	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
243	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
244	  \]
245	  and a canonical braiding
246	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
247	  inherited from the corresponding data on
248	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
249	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
254	  \leanok
255	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
256	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
257	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
258	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
259	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
260	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
261	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
262	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
263	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
264	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
265	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
266	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
267	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
268	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
269	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{theorem}
273	
274	\leanok
275	[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
276	  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
278	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
279	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
280	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
281	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
282	  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
283	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
284	  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
285	  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
286	  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
287	\end{theorem}
288	
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
291	  \leanok
292	  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
293	  are inherited from the analogous identities on
294	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
295	  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
296	  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
297	  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
298	  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
299	  identities yield global coherence identities on
300	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
301	  same affine descent argument.
302	\end{proof}

304	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
305	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
306	
307	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
308	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
309	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
310	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
311	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
312	monoidal-category structure of
313	\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
314	the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).
315	
316	\begin{lemma}
317	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
318	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
319	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
320	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
321	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
322	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
323	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
324	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
325	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
326	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	\begin{proof}
330	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
331	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
332	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
333	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
334	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
335	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
336	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
337	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
338	\end{proof}
339	
340	\begin{lemma}
341	[Inverse of an invertible module]
342	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
343	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
344	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
345	  bundle. The dual
346	  \[
347	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
348	  \]
349	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
350	  isomorphism
351	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
352	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
353	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
354	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
355	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
356	\end{lemma}
357	
358	\begin{proof}
359	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
360	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
361	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
362	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
363	  The contraction map
364	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
365	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
366	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
367	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
368	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
369	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
370	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	\begin{lemma}
374	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
375	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
376	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
377	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
378	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
379	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
380	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
381	  \[
382	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
383	    \;\;=\;\;
384	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
385	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
386	  \]
387	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
388	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
389	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
390	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
391	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
392	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
393	\end{lemma}
394	
395	\begin{proof}
396	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
397	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
398	  Direct from
399	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
400	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
401	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
402	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
403	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
404	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
405	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
406	  monoidal-category structure of
407	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
408	  the same affine descent argument.
409	\end{proof}
410	
411	\begin{lemma}
412	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
413	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
414	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
415	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
416	  functor
417	  \[
418	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
419	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
420	  \]
421	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
422	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
423	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
424	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
425	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
426	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
427	  canonical isomorphism
428	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
429	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
430	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
431	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
432	\end{lemma}
433	
434	\begin{proof}
435	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
436	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
437	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
438	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
439	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
440	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
441	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
442	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
443	  same descent argument as in
444	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
445	  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
446	  same cover.
447	\end{proof}
448	
449	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
450	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
451	
452	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
453	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
454	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
455	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
456	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
457	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
458	
459	\begin{theorem}
460	
461	\leanok
462	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
463	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
464	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
465	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
466	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
467	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
468	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
469	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
470	
471	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
472	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
473	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
474	  % groups)
475	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
476	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
477	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
478	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
479	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
480	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
481	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
482	  % \end{dfn}
483	  %  [\dots]
484	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
485	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
486	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
487	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
488	  % topologies  by
489	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
490	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
491	  %  \end{dfn}"
492	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
493	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
494	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
495	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
496	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
497	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
498	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
499	  \[
500	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
501	  \]
502	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
503	  abelian-group structure with addition
504	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
505	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
506	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
507	  inverse
508	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
509	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
510	  supplied by the substrate of
511	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
512	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
513	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
514	  map
515	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
516	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
517	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
518	\end{theorem}
519	
520	\begin{proof}
521	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
522	  \leanok
523	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
524	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
525	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
526	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
527	  By
528	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
529	  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
530	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
531	  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
532	  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
533	  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
534	  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
535	  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
536	  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
537	  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
538	  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).
539	
540	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
541	  functor
542	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
543	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
544	  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
545	  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
546	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
547	  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
548	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).
549	
550	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
551	  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
552	  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
553	  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
554	  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
555	  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
556	  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
557	  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
